back:
Established JUDICIAL REVIEW — courts can declare laws unconstitutional.

Result: Unanimous. Marbury had a right to commission, but SCOTUS couldn't order its delivery.
Constitutional Basis: Article III
Significance: Made SCOTUS the final authority on interpreting the Constitution.

Expanded federal power through IMPLIED POWERS.

Result: Unanimous for McCulloch. MD couldn't tax the national bank.
Constitutional Basis: Elastic Clause + Supremacy Clause
Significance: Federal government can use implied powers to expand beyond enumerated powers.

Established the CLEAR AND PRESENT DANGER test.

Result: Unanimous against Schenck. Draft-evasion fliers not protected.
Constitutional Basis: 1st Amendment (Free Speech)
Significance: Speech that incites violence/danger during wartime is NOT protected.

Overturned SEPARATE BUT EQUAL (Plessy v. Ferguson).

Result: Unanimous for Brown. Segregated schools are inherently unequal.
Constitutional Basis: 14th Amendment (Equal Protection)
Significance: Dismantled legal basis for racial segregation in public schools.

Established ONE MAN, ONE VOTE principle.

Result: 6-2 for Baker. Federal courts can review redistricting.
Constitutional Basis: Article III + 14th Amendment
Significance: Ended courts deferring to states on redistricting; urban votes must equal rural votes.

School-sponsored prayer violates the ESTABLISHMENT CLAUSE.

Result: 6-1 for Engel. State-composed prayer banned.
Constitutional Basis: 1st Amendment (Establishment Clause)
Significance: Establishment Clause covers ALL broad government religious activity, not just state churches.

States must provide lawyers to ALL felony defendants.

Result: Unanimous for Gideon. Overturned Betts v. Brady.
Constitutional Basis: 6th Amendment + 14th Amendment
Significance: Right to counsel extended to all felony cases in state courts.

Students retain FREE SPEECH rights in public schools.

Result: 7-2 for Tinker. Black armbands = protected speech.
Constitutional Basis: 1st Amendment (Free Speech)
Key Test: School may only restrict speech causing 'material and substantial disruption.'

PRIOR RESTRAINT of the press is unconstitutional.

Result: 6-3 for NY Times. Pentagon Papers could be published.
Constitutional Basis: 1st Amendment (Freedom of Press)
Significance: Government cannot censor press before publication except in most extreme circumstances.

FREE EXERCISE CLAUSE incorporated to states.

Result: 7-0 for Yoder. Amish families exempted from HS attendance.
Constitutional Basis: 1st Amendment Free Exercise + 14th Amendment
Significance: States must show compelling interest to limit religious practice.

Right to PRIVACY protects abortion in 1st trimester.

Result: 7-2 for Roe. Texas abortion ban struck down.
Constitutional Basis: 14th Amendment Due Process + 9th Amendment
Note: Overturned by Dobbs v. Jackson (2022).

Racial GERRYMANDERING violates Equal Protection.

Result: 5-4 for Shaw. NC majority-minority district challenged.
Constitutional Basis: 14th Amendment (Equal Protection)
Significance: Equal Protection extends to majority groups; basis for challenging affirmative action.

Limits Congress's COMMERCE CLAUSE power.

Result: 5-4 for Lopez. Gun Free School Zones Act struck down.
Constitutional Basis: Commerce Clause + 10th Amendment
Significance: Carrying a gun at school is NOT an economic activity; major win for states' rights.

Corporations have FREE SPEECH rights in elections.

Result: 5-4 for Citizens United. BCRA limits struck down.
Constitutional Basis: 1st Amendment (Free Speech)
Significance: Unlimited independent political spending by corporations/unions → rise of Super PACs.

2nd Amendment INCORPORATED to state governments.

Result: 5-4 for McDonald. Chicago handgun ban struck down.
Constitutional Basis: 2nd Amendment + 14th Amendment
Significance: States cannot severely restrict private gun ownership.

Author: Thomas Jefferson | Context: 2nd Continental Congress

Influenced by: John Locke (social contract, natural rights)

3 Parts:
1. Declaration of Human Rights
2. List of 27 grievances against King George
3. Declaration of Independence from Britain

Key idea: Government derives power from consent of governed; people may overthrow a government that violates the contract.

America's first government after the Revolution.

Key Structure:
• Unicameral Congress, 1 vote per state
• No executive or judicial branch

Fatal Weaknesses:
• Could NOT tax (only request money)
• No national court system
• Could not enforce laws on states
• 9/13 states needed to pass laws
• Unanimous vote to amend

Collapsed after Shays' Rebellion (1786)

Author: James Madison (Virginia Plan basis)

6 Principles: Popular sovereignty, Federalism, Judicial review, Checks & balances, Separation of powers, Limited government

3 Parts: Preamble (6 goals) | 7 Articles | 27 Amendments

Articles: I=Legislative, II=Executive, III=Judicial, IV=State relations, V=Amendments, VI=Supremacy, VII=Ratification

Author: Robert Yates (pseudonym 'Brutus')
Position: AGAINST ratifying the Constitution

Main Arguments:
• Country too LARGE & diverse for a single Republic
• Elastic Clause could justify any law Congress wanted
• Supremacy Clause would crush state sovereignty
• Congress's tax power has no limit
• No Bill of Rights to protect individual liberties
• Standing army is a threat to freedom

Purpose: Defend the Constitution by arguing a LARGE REPUBLIC controls factions better than a small one.

Faction = group united by common interest harmful to others

Solutions to factions:
1. A Republic (filters out bad ideas through representatives)
2. A LARGE Republic (no single faction can form a 51% majority)
3. Separation of powers

Key quote: 'Liberty is to faction what air is to fire.'

Purpose: Defend the Constitution's system of CHECKS & BALANCES against Anti-Federalist fears.

Anti-tyranny mechanisms:
1. Separation of powers (independent branches)
2. Checks & balances (each can push back on others)
3. Bicameralism (two-house legislature checks itself)
4. Federalism (national vs. state governments check each other)

Key quotes:
• 'Ambition must counteract ambition.'
• 'If men were angels, no government would be necessary.'

Purpose: Defend a SINGLE, STRONG EXECUTIVE (one president).

Why one president is better than a group:
• Faster decisions (energy, speed)
• No internal disagreement or leaks
• Easier to hold accountable — clear who to blame
• Can be re-elected or removed

4 qualities needed: Unity, Duration, Adequate support, Competent powers

Key quote: 'Energy in the executive is essential to good government.'

Purpose: Defend JUDICIAL INDEPENDENCE and lifetime tenure.

Key arguments:
• Judiciary is 'least dangerous' branch (no force, no will — only judgment)
• Judges need lifetime tenure to stay independent from political pressure
• Courts are the guardians of the Constitution
• Courts must declare unconstitutional acts VOID (→ judicial review)

Key quote: '[Courts have] neither FORCE nor WILL, but merely judgment.'

front: Letter from Birmingham Jail (1963) — Dr. MLK Jr.
back:
Author: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. | Written from Birmingham City Jail

Addressed to: White moderate clergy who called him an 'outside agitator'

4 Steps of nonviolent campaign:
1. Collect facts (confirm injustice)
2. Negotiate with leaders
3. Self-purification (train to resist without retaliation)
4. Direct action (marches, sit-ins)

Just law: majority and minority both must follow
Unjust law: majority imposes on minority without following it themselves

Key quote: 'Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere.'

front: 4 Theories on the Origin of Government
back:
1. Divine Right — leaders chosen by God (monarchies)
2. Evolutionary — government grew out of the family unit
3. Force Theory — a person/group takes power by force
4. Social Contract Theory — an agreement among people to create order (Locke, Rousseau)

America is based on SOCIAL CONTRACT THEORY

HOBBES: Man is brutal by nature; need strong ruler; surrender rights for order

LOCKE: Social contract; consent of governed; people can overthrow violating ruler (→ DOI)

ROUSSEAU: Contract among the people; majority vote; minority must accept

MONTESQUIEU: Separation of powers + checks & balances limits government (→ Constitution)

UNITARY: One central government holds all power (England, most countries)

FEDERAL: Power SHARED between national and state governments, each with own sovereignty (USA today)

CONFEDERACY: Member states hold the power; weak national government for limited purposes (USA under Articles of Confederation, Confederate States)

1. English rule + debt → taxes on colonies
2. Colonies resist → 1st and 2nd Continental Congress
3. Declaration of Independence (1776)
4. Win Revolution → Articles of Confederation (1781)
5. AOC weaknesses exposed by Shays' Rebellion (1786)
6. Constitutional Convention (1787) in Philadelphia
7. Virginia Plan vs. New Jersey Plan
8. Connecticut Compromise → bicameral Congress
9. Federalists vs. Anti-Federalists (Ratification debate)
10. Constitution ratified + Bill of Rights added (1791)

Article I — Legislative Branch (makes laws)
Article II — Executive Branch (enforces laws)
Article III — Judicial Branch (interprets laws)
Article IV — Relations among states (Full Faith & Credit, Extradition, Privileges & Immunities)
Article V — Amendment process
Article VI — Supremacy Clause (federal law is supreme)
Article VII — Ratification process

1st — Speech, Press, Petition, Assembly, Religion*
2nd — Right to bear arms*
3rd — No quartering soldiers
4th — No unreasonable searches/seizures*
5th — Grand jury, no double jeopardy, self-incrimination, due process, eminent domain*
6th — Speedy trial, lawyer, confront witnesses*
7th — Jury trial in civil cases
8th — No excessive bail/fines, no cruel & unusual punishment*
9th — Unenumerated rights retained by people*
10th — Reserved powers to states*

* = Most AP-tested

DUAL FEDERALISM (Layer Cake) — pre-1930s
• Each level has clearly separate, distinct powers
• Little overlap between national and state roles

COOPERATIVE FEDERALISM (Marble Cake) — post-New Deal
• National and state governments share programs, costs, and administration
• Federal grants come with strings (guidelines, non-discrimination provisions)

FISCAL FEDERALISM (1970s–present)
• Federal influence through grant system
• Categorical grants, block grants, and mandates

Civil Liberties = protections FROM government (Bill of Rights)
Civil Rights = protections AGAINST discrimination

Incorporation = applying Bill of Rights to states via 14th Amendment Due Process Clause

Protections already in Constitution (before BOR):
• Habeas corpus
• No bills of attainder
• No ex post facto laws

Right to privacy = implied by 1st, 3rd, 4th, 9th, 14th (not stated directly)

HOUSE (435 members, 2-yr terms):
• Revenue bills originate here
• Power to impeach
• Rules Committee controls all floor debate
• Elects president if no one gets 270 EV
• Qualifications: 25 yrs old, 7-yr citizen

SENATE (100 members, 6-yr terms):
• Confirms appointments (51%)
• Approves treaties (2/3)
• Tries impeachment cases
• Unlimited debate → filibuster → cloture (60 votes)
• Qualifications: 30 yrs old, 9-yr citizen

topic: How a Bill Becomes a Law
front: How a Bill Becomes a Law (HABBAL)
back:
1. IDEA from anyone
2. INTRODUCED in House ($) or Senate
3. COMMITTEE (subcommittee: hearings, markup, vote)
4. RULES COMMITTEE (House) / Majority Leader (Senate) schedules floor vote
5. FLOOR DEBATE & VOTE
6. OTHER CHAMBER repeats process
7. If different versions → CONFERENCE COMMITTEE
8. Both chambers vote on final version
9. TO PRESIDENT:
  • Sign = Law
  • Veto = Dead (Congress can override 2/3 each chamber)
  • Pocket Veto = Dead (Congress adjourned, 10 days)
  • Pocket Pass = Law (Congress in session, 10 days, no action)

ENUMERATED (Delegated) Powers:
• Tax and spend • Regulate commerce • Declare war • Coin money • Raise army/navy

IMPLIED Powers (via Elastic Clause):
• Any law 'necessary and proper' to carry out enumerated powers

INHERENT Powers (all national governments have):
• Control borders, defend against revolution, recognize foreign governments

CHECKS:
• Override veto (2/3) • Confirm appointments • Approve treaties (2/3) • Impeach • Power of purse • Oversight

1. CHIEF EXECUTIVE — enforce laws; executive orders; pardon/reprieve
2. CHIEF LEGISLATOR — propose bills; veto; State of the Union
3. PARTY CHIEF — lead party; raise funds; patronage
4. CHIEF DIPLOMAT — foreign policy; treaties; executive agreements; recognize governments
5. CHIEF OF STATE — ceremonial head; represents nation
6. COMMANDER IN CHIEF — head of military; war decisions; War Powers Act
7. CHIEF ECONOMIC PLANNER — budget request; economic reports; OMB

POWERS: Executive orders, Commander in Chief, Treaties/Exec. agreements, Veto, Pardon/reprieve, Appoint judges/cabinet, Recess appointments, Special session, State of the Union

LIMITS:
• Appointments need Senate approval (51%)
• Treaties need Senate approval (2/3)
• Vetoes overridden by 2/3 each chamber
• Impeachment (House: 51%, Senate: 2/3)
• 22nd Amendment (2-term limit)
• War Powers Resolution (60-day limit on troop deployment without Congress)

1. CABINET DEPARTMENTS (15) — enforce laws; head called Secretary (except Attorney General); discretionary power

2. INDEPENDENT EXECUTIVE AGENCIES — own mission, govt funding (NASA, CIA)

3. INDEPENDENT REGULATORY COMMISSIONS — regulate specific economic sectors (FCC, FDA, FEC, SEC)

4. GOVERNMENT CORPORATIONS — govt-owned businesses, partially funded by govt (USPS, AMTRAK, TVA)

IRON TRIANGLE: Congressional committee ↔ Bureaucratic agency ↔ Special interest group

94 DISTRICT COURTS — original jurisdiction, 1 judge, 300-400k cases/yr

12 COURTS OF APPEALS — appellate jurisdiction, 3-judge panels, 50-60k cases/yr

1 SUPREME COURT — 9 justices; 1% original, 99% appellate
• Cert granted by Rule of Four
• Less than 1% of petitions accepted
• Term: Oct 1 – Sept 30
• Justices appointed by president, confirmed by Senate, serve for life

Judicial tools: Judicial review, Interpreting law, Reversing own decisions

13th Amendment (1865) — Abolished slavery
14th Amendment (1868) — Citizenship, due process, equal protection
15th Amendment (1870) — Right to vote regardless of race

Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) — Separate but equal
Brown v. Board (1954) — Overturned separate but equal

Civil Rights Act 1964 — Banned discrimination in employment & public accommodations
Voting Rights Act 1965 — Banned discriminatory voting practices

Martin Luther King Jr. — Nonviolent direct action (sit-ins, marches, boycotts, Letter from Birmingham Jail)

1st — Religion, Speech, Press, Assembly, Petition
2nd — Bear arms
4th — Search & seizure
5th — Grand jury, self-incrimination, due process
6th — Right to counsel, speedy trial
8th — Cruel & unusual punishment
9th — Unenumerated rights
10th — Reserved powers to states
13th — Abolished slavery
14th — Citizenship, equal protection, due process (incorporation!)
15th — Vote regardless of race
16th — Income tax
17th — Direct election of senators
19th — Women's suffrage
22nd — Presidential term limits
24th — No poll tax
25th — Presidential disability
26th — Vote at 18
27th — Congressional pay delay
